lines.text: Here is the honest truth about almost every hand you play: both of you have a range.
Your opponent does not know your exact cards, and you do not know theirs.
That means every decision you make is a range decision, whether you think about it that way or not.
Good players bluff because their range demands it — not just because they have a weak hand.
Good players also bluff-catch because their range demands it — not just because they feel suspicious.
When you fixate on your own two cards, you are thinking about only half of the equation.
The player who thinks in ranges is always asking what their betting line looks like across all their hands.
They ask what story their range is telling, and whether that story is consistent.
This is why a strong player can bet nothing with the nuts and still be doing the right thing.
The right play comes from what your whole range needs, not from what your specific hand needs.
Reciprocal thinking is what separates hand-readers from range-managers.
